Home / Unit Economics Calculator

Unit Economics Calculator

Analyze per-customer profitability: gross margin, CAC payback, LTV, and how to optimize your unit economics.

How to Use This Tool

1
Enter your average monthly revenue per customer.
2
Enter your monthly cost to serve one customer (hosting, support, API costs).
3
Enter your Customer Acquisition Cost (total sales & marketing spend ÷ new customers).
4
Enter your monthly churn rate as a percentage.
5
Check the Per-Customer Snapshot for gross profit, margin, and LTV.
6
Review CAC Payback to see how quickly you recover acquisition costs.
7
Scan Scaling Economics to see how unit profit scales with customer count.
8
Read Optimization Levers to see how small improvements compound.
9
tools.solopreneur-unit-economics-calculator.how_to_use.8
10
tools.solopreneur-unit-economics-calculator.how_to_use.9
11
tools.solopreneur-unit-economics-calculator.how_to_use.10

Frequently Asked Questions

What is unit economics?

Unit economics breaks down your business to the per-customer level: how much revenue each customer generates, what it costs to serve them, and how much you spend to acquire them. Positive unit economics means each customer is profitable on their own — the foundation of a sustainable business.

What is a good gross margin?

For SaaS, 70-90% gross margin is typical. For e-commerce, 40-60%. For services/agencies, 20-40%. If your gross margin is under 50%, look for ways to automate support, reduce hosting costs, or raise prices.

What is a good CAC payback period?

Under 6 months is healthy, under 3 months is excellent. Over 12 months means you need a lot of capital to grow. Most SaaS investors want to see payback under 12 months.

How does churn affect unit economics?

Churn caps your LTV. Even with great margins, if customers leave after 5 months and it takes 6 months to recover CAC, you lose money on every customer. This calculator shows exactly how much extra LTV you gain by reducing churn by just 25%.

Should I raise prices or reduce costs?

Raising prices has the biggest leverage — a 20% price increase flows directly to gross profit. Reducing cost-to-serve (automation, better infra) compounds with scale. Doing both is ideal. See the Optimization Levers section for concrete numbers.

tools.solopreneur-unit-economics-calculator.faq.5.q

Results

Copy All
Example

📦 Unit Economics

📋 Per-Customer Snapshot • Revenue / Customer: $50/mo • Expansion Revenue: $15/mo • Cost to Serve: $10/mo • Net Contribution: $55/mo (84.6% margin) • Monthly Churn: 3.0% • Avg Customer Lifetime: 33 months • Customer LTV: $1,833 ⏱️ CAC Payback • Cost to Acquire: $200 • Monthly Net Contribution: $55/mo • Payback Period: 3.6 months • Annual Profit: $660 per customer • 🟢 Good — payback under 6 months. Healthy unit economics. 📊 Scaling Economics • 1,000 customers: $55,000/mo net → $660,000/yr (acquire: $206,000) • 10,000 customers: $560,000/mo net → $6,720,000/yr (cost -10%) (acquire: $2,060,000) • 100,000 customers: $5,700,000/mo net → $68,400,000/yr (cost -20%) (acquire: $20,600,000) ^ Economies of scale: 10% cost reduction per 10× customers 🎯 Optimization Levers • Reduce churn 25%: LTV $1,833 → $2,444 (+$611) • Raise price 20%: Net $55/mo → $65/mo | Payback 4 → 3 mo • Reduce CAC 30%: Payback 4 → 3 mo (CAC: $200 → $140) • 🚀 Best case (lower churn + lower CAC): LTV $2,444 | Payback 3 mo 🏆 Lever Impact Ranking • Reduce churn 1%: LTV $1,833 → $2,750 (+$917) • +10% Expansion: LTV $1,833 → $1,883 (+$50) 🥇 Top Lever: Reduce Churn (+$917 LTV vs +$50 from expansion)
